Introduction

Imagine a student who writes answers only from what they remember. Sometimes theyΓÇÖll be right, sometimes theyΓÇÖll guess, and sometimes theyΓÇÖll confidently say the wrong thing.

Now imagine the same student is allowed to open a book, find the right page, and then explain the answer. ThatΓÇÖs how RAG Retrieval means finding the right information. Generation means explaining it in natural language.

So, RAG is not magic, itΓÇÖs just a good habit: donΓÇÖt talk first; look it up first.

How does a standard LLM answer:

A standard LLM answers from what it already knows, its trained weights. Think of it like a student in an exam hall whoΓÇÖs not allowed to open a book. If they remember the concept, great. If the fact is new or specific, they might guess, or theyΓÇÖll say they donΓÇÖt know. RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) changes the workflow.

It first looks up relevant information from a trusted knowledge source (documents, PDFs, wiki pages, policies, tickets, databases), then answers using that retrieved context, like a student who is allowed to consult textbooks before writing the final answer.

Query: ΓÇ£What were AppleΓÇÖs Q4 2024 revenue figures?ΓÇ¥

  • LLM-only: might be outdated (training cutoffs are real) ΓåÆ it may guess or refuse.
  • RAG: retrieves the latest earnings report snippet ΓåÆ answers accurately based on retrieved evidence.

HereΓÇÖs the key mindset:

  • LLM-only = speak from memory
  • RAG = search first, then speak

Pure LLMs commonly struggle with four practical problems:

Static knowledge

A normal LLM is like a textbook printed last year. If something changed after it was printed, the book wonΓÇÖt magically update.

What this looks like in real life:

  • A company updates its remote work policy
  • A product gets a new feature
  • A price changes due to inflation or discounts
  • A government rule changes

Example: Your company changes leave policy from 15 days ΓåÆ 20 days starting Jan 1, 2025.

  • LLM-only answer: might still say ΓÇ£15 daysΓÇ¥ because that was true earlier (or because itΓÇÖs guessing).
  • RAG answer: pulls the latest HR handbook and says: ΓÇ£ItΓÇÖs 20 days from Jan 1, 2025ΓÇ¥ (and can point to the exact line).

Hallucinations

When an LLM doesnΓÇÖt know the exact answer, it sometimes fills the gap with something that sounds right, like a student making up an answer because they donΓÇÖt want to leave the page blank.

ItΓÇÖs not ΓÇ£lyingΓÇ¥ on purpose. ItΓÇÖs doing what it was trained to do: produce a fluent continuation.

User asks: ΓÇ£WhatΓÇÖs our refund policy for annual subscriptions?ΓÇ¥

If the LLM canΓÇÖt see your real policy, it may produce something like: ΓÇ£You can get a refund within 30 days.ΓÇ¥

It sounds believableΓÇª but your actual policy might be:

  • ΓÇ£No refunds on annual plansΓÇ¥ or ΓÇ£Refunds only within 7 daysΓÇ¥ or ΓÇ£Refunds allowed only if service wasnΓÇÖt usedΓÇ¥

RAG fixes this by forcing the model to answer using your real policy text, not imagination.

3. No source attribution

Even if an LLM gives the right answer, it usually canΓÇÖt tell you where the answer came fromΓÇölike someone saying ΓÇ£trust meΓÇ¥ without showing the document.
That becomes a big problem in professional settings where you need: auditability, compliance,

trust, accuracy checks

Example:

You ask: ΓÇ£WhatΓÇÖs the SLA for P1 incidents?ΓÇ¥

  • LLM replies: ΓÇ£The SLA is 1 hour.ΓÇ¥

Now your next question is naturally: ΓÇ£Where is that written?ΓÇ¥

LLM-only canΓÇÖt point you to the internal PDF or wiki section (because it didnΓÇÖt retrieve it).

But RAG can respond like: ΓÇ£SLA for P1 incidents is 1 hour (from Incident SOP ΓåÆ Section 2.1).ΓÇ¥ So, the user can verify it.

4. Domain ignorance

A public LLM doesnΓÇÖt automatically know your companyΓÇÖs internal dataΓÇöyour HR handbook, product docs, client contracts, Jira tickets, dashboards, or emails. That information simply isnΓÇÖt part of the public training set. So, expecting the LLM to answer internal questions without retrieval is like asking a stranger: ΓÇ£What did my manager say in yesterdayΓÇÖs meeting?ΓÇ¥ They can only guess.

Example: User asks: ΓÇ£How do we deploy the app to production in our company?ΓÇ¥

  • LLM-only: gives a generic answer (Docker/Kubernetes/CI/CD) but may be wrong for your setup.
  • RAG: retrieves your internal runbook and answers exactly: ΓÇ£Merge to main ΓåÆ pipeline triggers ΓåÆ approval step ΓåÆ deploy to GKE ΓåÆ verify logs.ΓÇ¥

What RAG does differently

  • Pulls live / updatable documents
  • Bases answers on retrieved evidence
  • Can attach citations to specific passages
  • Works naturally with proprietary data (HR docs, SOPs, internal wikis, PDFs)

Example:

User: ΓÇ£WhatΓÇÖs our companyΓÇÖs policy on remote work in 2025?ΓÇ¥

  • LLM alone: might generate a generic policy (hallucination risk)
  • RAG: retrieves your HR handbook section ΓåÆ answers with the exact policy + citation

RAG vs Retraining

People often ask: ΓÇ£If knowledge changes, why not just retrain the model?ΓÇ¥. Because retraining is heavy. RAG is lightweight. Retrain an LLM (expensive + slow)

Scenario: vacation policy changes from 15 ΓåÆ 20 days on Jan 1, 2025

Typical retrain flow:

  1. Collect training data
  2. Add the updated policy document
  3. Retrain (or fine-tune) a huge model
  4. Validate, safety-check, deploy

ThatΓÇÖs time, cost, risk, and operational complexity.

RAG update (fast + practical)

  1. Upload the new HR handbook PDF
  2. System auto-chunks + creates embeddings
  3. Stores new chunks in the vector DB

Result: the system starts answering with updated policy without touching the LLM.

RAG decouples knowledge from the model. Update the knowledge base ΓåÆ answers update immediately.

Real-world Examples

  • Banking Customer Support (e.g., Indian banks like HDFC): RAG pulls latest RBI circulars + internal policies for loan queries ΓåÆ accurate with citations, no outdated guesses.
  • Legal Research: Law firms retrieve exact case laws from thousands of judgments ΓåÆ avoids fabricated precedents.
  • E-commerce Support: Queries like ΓÇ£current festive discount policyΓÇ¥ pull real-time pricing docs ΓåÆ prevents wrong offers from static LLM knowledge.

Code Implementation (Simple Python RAG starter using LangChain + OpenAI)

from langchain.document_loaders import PyPDFLoader

from langchain.text_splitter import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

from langchain.embeddings import OpenAIEmbeddings

from langchain.vectorstores import FAISS

from langchain.chains import RetrievalQA

from langchain.llms import OpenAI

# 1. Ingest & Index (your HR handbook PDF)

loader = PyPDFLoader("hr_handbook_2025.pdf")

docs = loader.load()

text_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=1000, chunk_overlap=200)

chunks = text_splitter.split_documents(docs)

# 2. Create vector store

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings()

vectorstore = FAISS.from_documents(chunks, embeddings)

# 3. RAG Chain

llm = OpenAI(temperature=0)

qa_chain = RetrievalQA.from_chain_type(

llm=llm,

chain_type="stuff",

retriever=vectorstore.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 5}),

return_source_documents=True

)

# Ask question

result = qa_chain({"query": "What is our 2025 remote work policy?"})

print(result["result"])

print("\nSources:", [doc.metadata for doc in result["source_documents"]])

Mermaid Flowchart (LLM-only vs RAG)

Quizzes

Module 1: Introduction to RAG

Easy Questions

  1. What does RAG stand for?
    A) Random Access Generator
    B) Retrieval-Augmented Generation
    C) Real-time Answer Generator
    D) Reinforced AI Guidance
  2. Which of the following is a common problem with standard LLMs (without RAG)?
    A) They can easily access up-to-date private company data
    B) They sometimes hallucinate answers
    C) They always provide source citations
    D) Their knowledge updates automatically every day
  3. In a standard RAG workflow, what happens immediately after the user asks a question? A) The LLM generates an answer directly from its training data
    B) Relevant documents/chunks are retrieved from a vector database
    C) The model is retrained with new information
    D) The answer is output without any lookup
  4. What does the document compare a standard LLM to?
    A) A student who is allowed to open a book
    B) A student in an exam hall who is not allowed to open a book
    C) A calculator
    D) A search engine

Medium Questions

  1. Why is RAG generally preferred over retraining an LLM when company policies change? A) Retraining is faster and cheaper
    B) RAG allows immediate updates by simply adding new documents to the knowledge base
    C) Retraining eliminates all hallucinations completely
    D) RAG requires more computational resources
  2. What is one real-world example given for RAG in banking?
    A) Pulling latest RBI circulars + internal policies for loan queries
    B) Generating random stock prices
    C) Creating new customer accounts
    D) Training new models daily
  3. What problem does RAG solve regarding source attribution?
    A) It can attach citations to specific passages
    B) It removes all citations
    C) It makes answers longer
    D) It only works for public data

Hard Questions

  1. What is the key mindset difference between LLM-only and RAG?
    A) LLM-only = search first, then speak; RAG = speak from memory
    B) LLM-only = speak from memory; RAG = search first, then speak
    C) Both are the same
    D) RAG never uses memory
  2. In the simple Python RAG code example, which library is used for the vector store?
    A) Chroma
    B) FAISS
    C) Pinecone
    D) Weaviate
  3. What is the main benefit of RAG for domain-specific/internal company data?
    A) It works naturally with proprietary data like HR handbooks, SOPs, and internal wikis
    B) It makes the model larger
    C) It removes the need for any documents
    D) It only works for public websites

Answers for Module 1

  1. B
  2. B
  3. B
  4. B
  5. B
  6. A
  7. A
  8. B
  9. B
  10. A

Module 2: Beyond Naive RAG

Large language models (LLMs) are powerful but limited: they cannot access private or upΓÇætoΓÇædate information and they sometimes hallucinate. Enterprises therefore pair them with a retriever that fetches relevant documents so that answers are grounded in trustworthy sources. This pattern, retrieval followed by generation, is known as RAG. The RAG pipeline works as follows:

  1. Ingest and index: Documents are ingested, chunked and embedded. Each chunk is stored in a vector database.
  2. Retrieve: At query time, the userΓÇÖs question is embedded and compared to document embeddings using a similarity measure such as cosine similarity. The topΓÇæk chunks are returned.
  3. Augment and generate: The retrieved passages are concatenated to the question and sent to the LLM, which produces a response grounded in that context.

Cosine similarity, the most common scoring function, computes the angle between the query vector q and a document vector d_i as [formula placeholder ΓÇô original text shows math notation]

The retriever ranks documents by this score and the top results are passed to the generator.

Why naive RAG is insufficient

Naive RAG works well for simple factΓÇæbased questions but suffers from several recurring failure modes. A 2026 Neo4j white paper notes that vectorΓÇæonly retrieval misses rare tokens like IDs or codes; fixedΓÇæsize chunking splits tables or code blocks; oneΓÇæstage retrieval does not rerank results; missing filters allow offΓÇætopic documents into the prompt; limited query understanding underΓÇæretrieves for multi-hop questions; context windows are small; there is no feedback loop to detect weak evidence; and there is no graph context to connect entities and events. These problems cause irrelevant context, hallucinations and brittle performance.

Evolving beyond naive RAG

To address these shortcomings, engineers have developed a taxonomy of RAG variants. A 2026 Medium article lists eight architecturesΓÇöfrom naive to agenticΓÇöeach designed to fix a specific weakness. The most important variants are summarized below.

Hybrid RAG.

Combining dense vector search with lexical search (e.g., BM25) and, optionally, knowledge graphs cover both semantic similarity and exact token matching. Hybrid RAG merges ranked lists using reciprocal rank fusion (RRF),

where rank_k(d_i) is document d_iΓÇÖs rank under the kth retriever. Hybrid retrieval improves recall of names and codes. After retrieval, a crossΓÇæencoder re-ranker further polishes the ordering. Hybrid RAG is now the default pattern for production systems because it balances speed and accuracy.

Multimodal RAG.

RealΓÇæworld knowledge bases include images, audio and video. Multimodal RAG embeds these modalities into a shared space using models like CLIP or ImageBind so that text queries can retrieve images and vice versa. For example, a customerΓÇæsupport bot might return a video clip that demonstrates how to fix a printer, along with stepΓÇæbyΓÇæstep text instructions.

Graph RAG

(multiΓÇæhop RAG). When the information sought spans multiple documents or involves relationships, a knowledge graph provides context that vector search cannot. By modelling entities and relationships (people, products, citations), GraphRAG retrieves not just similar text but entire connected subgraphs. A legal firm, for instance, can use GraphRAG to discover patterns across intellectualΓÇæproperty cases by connecting companies, patents and court rulings.

HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings).

HyDE addresses the semantic gap by generating a hypothetical answer or question with an LLM and embedding that instead of the original query. Matching the hypothetical representation improves retrieval when the userΓÇÖs wording differs from the documents.

Adaptive RAG.

Not all queries are equal. Adaptive RAG classifies a questionΓÇÖs complexity and routes it to the appropriate pipeline: simple oneΓÇæhop retrieval, standard RAG with reranking, or multiΓÇæstep retrieval with decomposition. A complexity classifier identifies whether a question requires multi-hop reasoning; complex questions are broken into sub-queries, each retrieved independently, and the results are merged.

Corrective or Verified RAG.

Even with good retrieval, sometimes the context is weak. Corrective RAG (CRAG) adds a feedback loop: before answering, the system checks whether retrieved passages sufficiently support the question. If they donΓÇÖt, it triggers additional retrieval or stricter filters. It also instructs the LLM to answer only from the retrieved sources and to say ΓÇ£I donΓÇÖt knowΓÇ¥ if the answer is unsupported.

SelfΓÇæReflective RAG (SelfΓÇæRAG).

SelfΓÇæRAG uses selfΓÇæreflection tokens to evaluate the quality of retrieved context. During generation, the model critiques its own output for relevance, support and utility, deciding whether to retrieve more information before finalizing the answer. It dynamically performs

retrieval based on the content being generated, addressing the fixedΓÇæk retrieval issue in standard RAG.

Real-world Examples

  • Enterprise Search in Finance: Hybrid RAG combines vector + BM25 for querying transaction logs with rare IDs/codes ΓåÆ catches exact matches that pure vector misses.
  • Product Support with Images: Multimodal RAG in printer troubleshooting ΓåÆ text query retrieves video demo + step-by-step text from manual.
  • Legal Discovery: Graph RAG connects company names, patents, rulings across filings ΓåÆ uncovers hidden relationships in multi-document cases.
  • Customer FAQ Mismatch: HyDE generates hypothetical policy answer ΓåÆ embeds it to retrieve docs even if user phrasing differs from handbook.
  • Complex HR Query: Adaptive RAG detects multi-hop ("PTO accrual + carryover rules") ΓåÆ decomposes into sub-queries for better recall.

Code Implementation Snippet (Hybrid RAG with BM25 + Vector + Reranker using LangChain)

from langchain.retrievers import BM25Retriever, EnsembleRetriever

from langchain_community.retrievers import BM25Retriever

from langchain.vectorstores import FAISS

from langchain.embeddings import OpenAIEmbeddings

from langchain.retrievers.document_compressors import CrossEncoderReranker

from langchain_community.cross_encoders import HuggingFaceCrossEncoder

# Vector retriever (dense)

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings()

vector_retriever = FAISS.from_documents(docs, embeddings).as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 10})

# Keyword retriever (BM25)

bm25_retriever = BM25Retriever.from_documents(docs)

bm25_retriever.k = 10

# Ensemble (Hybrid)

ensemble_retriever = EnsembleRetriever(

retrievers=[bm25_retriever, vector_retriever],

weights=[0.4, 0.6] # Tune weights

)

# Reranker (cross-encoder)

reranker_model = HuggingFaceCrossEncoder(model_name="cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2")

compressor = CrossEncoderReranker(model=reranker_model, top_n=5)

reranked_retriever = ContextualCompressionRetriever(

base_compressor=compressor,

base_retriever=ensemble_retriever

)

# Use in chain

retrieved_docs = reranked_retriever.get_relevant_documents(query)

Quizzes

Module 2: Beyond Naive RAG

Easy Questions

  1. What is the first step in the standard RAG pipeline?
    A) Generate the answer
    B) Ingest and index documents (chunking + embedding)
    C) Call external tools
    D) Use a reranker
  2. What scoring function is most commonly used in naive RAG?
    A) Cosine similarity
    B) Euclidean distance only
    C) Jaccard similarity
    D) Hamming distance
  3. What does Hybrid RAG combine?
    A) Only vector search
    B) Dense vector search + lexical search (BM25) + reranking
    C) Only images and videos
    D) Only graph databases
  4. What does Multimodal RAG handle?
    A) Only text
    B) Images, audio, and video using shared embedding spaces
    C) Only code
    D) Only numbers

Medium Questions

  1. Why is naive RAG insufficient for production use?
    A) It always gives perfect answers
    B) It suffers from missed rare tokens, bad chunking, no reranking, etc.
    C) It is too slow
    D) It cannot handle text
  2. What is the purpose of a cross-encoder reranker in Hybrid RAG?
    A) To polish the ordering of retrieved documents
    B) To create embeddings
    C) To chunk documents
    D) To generate hypothetical answers
  3. What is Graph RAG useful for?
    A) Simple fact lookups
    B) Multi-hop questions and relationships using knowledge graphs
    C) Only keyword search
    D) Speed optimization only

Hard Questions

  1. What does HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) do?
    A) Generates a hypothetical answer/document and embeds that instead of the raw query
    B) Removes all documents
    C) Uses only BM25
    D) Calls web search every time
  2. What problem does Adaptive RAG solve?
    A) It routes questions based on complexity (simple vs multi-hop)
    B) It only handles images
    C) It removes reranking
    D) It makes everything slower
  3. Which of the following best describes Corrective RAG (CRAG) or Self-RAG?
    A) It adds a feedback loop to check if retrieved passages support the question and says ΓÇ£I donΓÇÖt knowΓÇ¥ if needed
    B) It focuses exclusively on image and video retrieval
    C) It is used only for simple one-hop questions
    D) It removes the need for any vector database

Answers for Module 2

  1. B
  2. A
  3. B
  4. B
  5. B
  6. A
  7. B
  8. A
  9. A
  10. A

Module 3: Agentic RAG

Agentic RAG and design patterns

Agentic RAG extends RAG with planning and tool use. Instead of a single fixed pipeline, an LLM acts as an agent that analyses the query, plans a strategy, calls various tools and iterates until satisfied. Agentic design patterns provide structured ways to implement such agents.

Reflection Pattern. The agent repeatedly critiques and refines its outputs, much like a human editor. It may generate a draft answer, review it for errors or missing information and then revise it. In SelfΓÇæRAG, reflection tokens help the model decide when to retrieve more information and assess the quality of generated text.

Tool Use Pattern. This pattern allows the agent to call external toolsΓÇödatabases, calculators, web search, code interpretersΓÇörather than relying solely on internal knowledge. For instance, it might fetch current stock prices or execute a Python function. Tool use turns the model into a dynamic problemΓÇæsolver.

Planning Pattern. Planning enables the agent to decompose a complex task into sub-tasks and sequence them strategically. It generates a roadmap of steps, such as gathering data, analysing results and synthesising conclusions. Frameworks like ReAct and ReWOO alternate between ΓÇ£thoughtΓÇ¥ and ΓÇ£action,ΓÇ¥ letting the agent reason about what to do next, choose the right retriever or tool, and adjust the plan based on new information.

MultiΓÇæAgent Pattern. In this pattern, multiple specialised agents collaborate. Some might focus on retrieval, others on analysis or writing. They communicate via a supervisor agent that coordinates tasks. This mirrors human teams and scales to large problems: for example, one agent extracts financial data while another evaluates HR policies, and a third synthesises the final report.

Agentic RAG uses these patterns to handle multiΓÇæstep questions. A Neo4j guide describes an agentic planning loop: break the question into concrete tasks (find entities, join relationships), choose the best tool for each (graph query, hybrid search, calculator), execute retrieval, verify coverage and conflicts, and stop when all subΓÇægoals are met. ChainΓÇæofΓÇæThought prompting serves as the agentΓÇÖs scratchpad, letting it outline its reasoning and track intermediate findings before producing a concise answer.

Real-world Examples

  • Financial Report Generation: Agent plans: (1) Retrieve internal Q4 revenue from DB, (2) Web search for industry benchmarks, (3) Calculate growth %, (4) Compare with competitors ΓåÆ final synthesized report with citations.
  • Complex HR + Compliance Query: ΓÇ£WhatΓÇÖs our 2025 leave policy and how does it align with new labor laws?ΓÇ¥ ΓåÆ Agent decomposes: retrieve internal handbook ΓåÆ search latest government rules ΓåÆ cross-check conflicts ΓåÆ draft answer with verification step.
  • Customer Support Escalation: Multi-agent setup ΓÇö one agent retrieves ticket history, another searches knowledge base, supervisor synthesizes response and decides if human handover is needed.
  • Research Assistant: Planning pattern breaks ΓÇ£Analyze 2025 AI regulation trendsΓÇ¥ into sub-tasks: search recent laws, retrieve company policy, graph entities (countries/companies), summarize implications

Code Implementation Snippet (Simple Agentic RAG with LangGraph / ReAct style using LangChain)

from langchain.agents import AgentExecutor, create_react_agent

from langchain.tools import Tool

from langchain.prompts import PromptTemplate

from langchain.llms import OpenAI

from langchain_community.tools import DuckDuckGoSearchRun

# Tools the agent can use

search = DuckDuckGoSearchRun()

retriever_tool = Tool(

name="Internal_RAG_Retriever",

func=lambda q: vectorstore.as_retriever().get_relevant_documents(q), # assume vectorstore from earlier

description="Search internal company documents for accurate policy/fact retrieval"

)

tools = [retriever_tool, search]

# ReAct-style prompt

prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(

"""Answer the following questions as best you can. You have access to the following tools:

{tools}

Use the following format:

Question: the input question you must answer

Thought: you should always think about what to do

Action: the action to take, should be one of [{tool_names}]

Action Input: the input to the action

Observation: the result of the action

... (this Thought/Action/Action Input/Observation can repeat N times)

Thought: I now know the final answer

Final Answer: the final answer to the original input question

Begin!

Question: {input}

{agent_scratchpad}"""

)

llm = OpenAI(temperature=0)

agent = create_react_agent(llm, tools, prompt)

agent_executor = AgentExecutor(agent=agent, tools=tools, verbose=True)

# Run complex multi-step query

response = agent_executor.invoke({

"input": "Compare our 2025 revenue growth with industry benchmarks and check if our PTO policy is competitive."

})

print(response["output"])

Mermaid Flowchart (Agentic RAG Planning Loop)

Quizzes

Module 3: Agentic RAG

Easy Questions

  1. What is Agentic RAG?
    A) A fixed single pipeline
    B) RAG extended with planning, tool use, and iteration by an LLM agent
    C) Only vector search
    D) Only keyword search
  2. Which pattern lets the agent critique and refine its own outputs?
    A) Reflection Pattern
    B) Static pipeline
    C) Only retrieval
    D) Only generation
  3. What does the Tool Use Pattern allow?
    A) Calling external tools like web search, calculators, or databases
    B) Nothing external
    C) Only internal memory
    D) Only chunking
  4. What is the Planning Pattern (e.g., ReAct)?
    A) Decomposing complex tasks into sub-tasks with thought-action loops
    B) Fixed k retrieval only
    C) No iteration
    D) Only reflection

Medium Questions

  1. In the Multi-Agent Pattern, what coordinates the agents?
    A) A supervisor agent
    B) No coordination
    C) Only one agent
    D) The user
  2. What does the code example for Agentic RAG use?
    A) create_react_agent and AgentExecutor
    B) Only RetrievalQA
    C) Only FAISS
    D) Only PyPDFLoader
  3. What is one real-world example of Agentic RAG?
    A) Financial report generation with multiple tool calls and synthesis
    B) Simple policy lookup
    C) Only image retrieval
    D) Only BM25 search

Hard Questions

  1. What does Chain-of-Thought serve as in Agentic RAG?
    A) The agentΓÇÖs scratchpad for reasoning
    B) Only the final answer
    C) Only embeddings
    D) Only chunk size
  2. In the agentic planning loop, what is the final step?
    A) Stop when all sub-goals are met
    B) Always retrieve more
    C) Never verify
    D) Ignore conflicts
  3. Which framework is mentioned for building Agentic workflows?
    A) LangGraph
    B) Only FAISS
    C) Only OpenAI
    D) Only PyPDF

Answers for Module 3

  1. B
  2. A
  3. A
  4. A
  5. A
  6. A
  7. A
  8. A
  9. A
  10. A

Module 4: Framework and Ecosystem Part

A growing ecosystem of frameworks helps developers build RAG systems. LangChain and LlamaIndex remain the most popular for orchestration; they offer modular pipelines, hybrid retrieval and integration with vector stores, knowledge graphs and agents. Dify provides visual dragΓÇæandΓÇædrop composition of RAG pipelines. RAGFlow emphasizes deep document understanding and advanced parsing. Pathway specializes in realΓÇætime data streaming. Flowlike is a lowΓÇæcode platform for connecting LLM components. Newer tools like LangGraph and LlamaIndexΓÇÖs agent modules support planΓÇôrouteΓÇôactΓÇôverify loops and multiΓÇæagent workflows.

Real-world Examples

  • LangChain in Production: Used by companies like IBM and startups for building customer support bots ΓÇö integrates Pinecone vector DB + hybrid search + agents for multi-step troubleshooting.
  • LlamaIndex for Enterprise Search: Adopted in legal/tech firms for indexing 100,000+ documents ΓÇö excels at node parsing, metadata filtering, and agentic query engines.
  • Dify for Non-Technical Teams: HR teams in mid-size companies use DifyΓÇÖs visual builder to create internal policy Q&A bots without writing code.
  • RAGFlow in Document-Heavy Industries: Manufacturing/insurance firms use it for deep parsing of PDFs with tables, figures, and nested structures.
  • LangGraph for Agentic Workflows: Teams building research assistants or financial analysts use LangGraph to define stateful graphs with cycles for reflection and tool retry.

Code Implementation Snippet (Quick LangChain + LlamaIndex Comparison Setup)

LangChain-style basic RAG:

from langchain.document_loaders import DirectoryLoader

from langchain.text_splitter import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

from langchain.vectorstores import Chroma

from langchain.embeddings import HuggingFaceEmbeddings

from langchain.chains import RetrievalQA

from langchain.llms import HuggingFaceHub

loader = DirectoryLoader("company_docs/")

docs = loader.load()

splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=800, chunk_overlap=150)

chunks = splitter.split_documents(docs)

embeddings = HuggingFaceEmbeddings(model_name="sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

vectorstore = Chroma.from_documents(chunks, embeddings)

llm = HuggingFaceHub(repo_id="mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2")

qa = RetrievalQA.from_chain_type(llm=llm, chain_type="stuff", retriever=vectorstore.as_retriever())

print(qa.run("What is our 2025 PTO policy?"))

LlamaIndex-style (more indexing power):

from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, SimpleDirectoryReader

from llama_index.embeddings.huggingface import HuggingFaceEmbedding

from llama_index.llms.huggingface import HuggingFaceLLM

documents = SimpleDirectoryReader("company_docs/").load_data()

embed_model = HuggingFaceEmbedding(model_name="BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5")

llm = HuggingFaceLLM(model_name="mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2")

index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(

documents,

embed_model=embed_model,

)

query_engine = index.as_query_engine(llm=llm)

response = query_engine.query("Summarize our remote work policy changes in 2025.")

print(response)

Mermaid Flowchart (Framework Comparison ΓÇô Orchestration Style)

  1. A
  2. A
  3. A
  4. A
  5. A
  6. A
  7. A
  8. A
  9. A
  10. A

Module 5: Building Production Level RAG

Building a robust RAG system means starting simple and then layering improvements. Stabilize basic retrieval with good embeddings, sensible chunking and a reranker to ensure highΓÇæquality context. Add hybrid search to catch rare tokens, introduce query expansion or HyDE to bridge wording gaps, and use parentΓÇæchild retrieval and summarization to fit more relevant context into the window. When your use cases involve relationships or global questions, model your data as a knowledge graph and use GraphRAG. For complex queries, implement agentic planning loops with planΓÇôrouteΓÇôactΓÇôverifyΓÇôstop structures. Finally, evaluate rigorously: measure retrieved context relevance, groundedness (faithfulness to sources), answer relevance and ranking metrics like Recall@k

Examples

Example 1:

Simple policy lookup. Suppose an employee asks, ΓÇ£What is our companyΓÇÖs paid-time-off (PTO) policy?ΓÇ¥ A naive RAG system converts the question into an embedding, retrieves the top five document chunks from the HR manual and feeds them into the LLM. It answers: ΓÇ£Employees accrue 1.5 days of PTO per month and must submit requests at least two weeks in advance.ΓÇ¥ This works because the question matches the document structure, and a single vector search is sufficient. However, if the query were phrased differently (ΓÇ£How do I request time off?ΓÇ¥), naive RAG might fail due to the semantic gap. A hybrid retriever using BM25 would catch keywords like ΓÇ£time off,ΓÇ¥ and a crossΓÇæencoder reranker would surface the correct chunk, improving accuracy.

Example 2:

Complex revenue comparison. Imagine a manager asks, ΓÇ£Compare our 2025 revenue growth with industry benchmarks and explain how our PTO policy compares to competitors.ΓÇ¥ This requires two separate information streams: financial data and HR policies. An agentic RAG system would plan the query in steps: (1) find revenue figures in the internal finance database, (2) search the web or industry reports for benchmarks, (3) retrieve company policy and competitor policies, (4) synthesise results and cite sources. The agent uses multiple tools: a database query engine for financial data, a web search tool for benchmarks and competitor policies, and an embedding retriever for internal policy documents. It iterates: after retrieving the first quarterΓÇÖs revenue, it realises it lacks Q4 data and issues another web search. The final answer blends numbers and qualitative analysis, with citations for each claim. Without agentic planning, a single retrieval step would likely miss some of this information or hallucinate the comparison.

Real-world Examples

  • Tech Company Internal Knowledge Base: Start with hybrid + reranker for employee handbook queries ΓåÆ add GraphRAG for connecting product features across docs ΓåÆ agentic for ΓÇ£How does this new feature affect compliance?ΓÇ¥
  • Financial Services Firm: Parent-child retrieval + summarization to handle long quarterly reports ΓåÆ evaluate with Recall@10 and groundedness score ΓåÆ catch hallucinations in compliance answers.
  • Healthcare Provider: Use HyDE for varied patient query phrasing (ΓÇ£What are side effects of this drug?ΓÇ¥ vs ΓÇ£Risks of medication XΓÇ¥) ΓåÆ agentic loop verifies against latest FDA updates.
  • E-commerce Platform: Real-time Pathway integration for live pricing docs ΓåÆ multi-agent for ΓÇ£Compare our discount policy with Amazon/FlipkartΓÇ¥ queries.

Code Implementation Snippet (Production-Grade Setup with Evaluation)

# Advanced RAG with hybrid, reranker, parent-child, and basic eval (using RAGAS or similar)

from langchain.retrievers import ContextualCompressionRetriever

from langchain.retrievers.document_compressors import LLMChainExtractor

from langchain_community.cross_encoders import HuggingFaceCrossEncoder

from langchain.chains import RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain

from ragas import evaluate

from ragas.metrics import faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_recall

# Parent-Document Retriever (for long docs)

from langchain.retrievers import ParentDocumentRetriever

from langchain.storage import InMemoryStore

from langchain.text_splitter import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

child_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=400)

parent_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=2000)

vectorstore = Chroma(...) # your vector db

store = InMemoryStore()

retriever = ParentDocumentRetriever(

vectorstore=vectorstore,

docstore=store,

child_splitter=child_splitter,

parent_splitter=parent_splitter,

)

# Reranker

compressor = CrossEncoderReranker(HuggingFaceCrossEncoder(model_name="cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2"))

compression_retriever = ContextualCompressionRetriever(

base_compressor=compressor,

base_retriever=retriever

)

# QA Chain with sources

qa_chain = RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain.from_chain_type(

llm=ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini"),

retriever=compression_retriever

)

# Evaluation (example with RAGAS)

testset = [...] # your test questions + ground truth

result = evaluate(

testset,

metrics=[faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_recall],

llm=ChatOpenAI(),

embeddings=OpenAIEmbeddings()

)

print(result)

Mermaid Flowchart (Production RAG Build Progression)

Conclusion: Bringing It All Together ΓÇô The Evolution of RAG in Practice

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has evolved far beyond a simple ΓÇ£lookup + generateΓÇ¥ trick into one of the most practical and powerful ways to make large language models reliable, up-to-date, and enterprise-ready in 2026.

We began with the core insight: pure LLMs are students relying only on memory powerful, but prone to outdated facts, confident hallucinations, lack of source transparency, and complete ignorance of private/company data. RAG changes the game by teaching the model the habit of looking things up first, grounding every answer in real, retrievable evidence.

The classic (ΓÇ£naiveΓÇ¥) RAG pipeline (ingest ΓåÆ chunk ΓåÆ embed ΓåÆ retrieve ΓåÆ augment ΓåÆ generate) already solves many pain points ΓÇö especially for static documents and straightforward fact lookups. But production reality quickly exposes its limits: missed rare tokens, bad chunk boundaries, irrelevant context, weak multi-hop reasoning, no quality feedback, and no relational awareness.

ThatΓÇÖs why the field rapidly advanced into a rich taxonomy of advanced variants:

  • Hybrid RAG became the production default by combining semantic vectors with keyword precision (BM25) and reranking ΓÇö dramatically improving recall on names, IDs, codes, and exact matches.
  • Multimodal RAG opened the door to images, diagrams, videos, and audio in knowledge bases.
  • Graph RAG unlocked connected, multi-document understanding through knowledge graphs.
  • HyDE, Adaptive, CRAG, Self-RAG addressed semantic gaps, query complexity, weak evidence, and fixed-k retrieval brittleness.

The most transformative leap came with Agentic RAG: transforming the system from a fixed pipeline into an intelligent agent that plans, reflects, uses tools, iterates, and collaborates across specialized sub-agents. This pattern handles the truly complex, multi-step, real-world questions that enterprises actually ask from cross-referencing internal data with live web benchmarks to verifying compliance across changing regulations.

TodayΓÇÖs ecosystem ΓÇö LangChain, LlamaIndex, LangGraph, Dify, RAGFlow, Pathway, Flowise ΓÇö gives developers modular, visual, and real-time building blocks to compose these systems quickly, while production best practices emphasize starting simple, layering improvements (hybrid ΓåÆ HyDE ΓåÆ parent-child ΓåÆ GraphRAG ΓåÆ agentic loops), and **rigorous evaluation** (context relevance, groundedness, answer quality, Recall@k).

In short:

RAG is no longer an optional enhancement - it is the standard way to deploy trustworthy, auditable, continuously updatable AI assistants in any serious organization.

Whether youΓÇÖre answering employee policy questions, generating financial comparisons, troubleshooting products with diagrams, or reasoning over interconnected legal cases, the path is clear:

1. Never speak from memory alone when truth matters.

2. Retrieve first, then reason.

3. Start naive, then evolve- hybrid, multimodal, graph, agentic - as complexity demands.

4. Evaluate relentlessly and keep the knowledge base fresh.

The student who once guessed is now the scholar with an open library, a calculator, the internet, and a team of specialists - all working together under one intelligent interface.

That is the real power of modern RAG in 2026.

Quizzes

Module 5: Building Production Level RAG

Easy Questions

  1. What is the recommended first step when building production RAG?
    A) Start simple with good embeddings, chunking, and a reranker
    B) Jump directly to agentic loops
    C) Use only graphs
    D) Skip evaluation
  2. What technique uses small ΓÇ£childΓÇ¥ chunks for retrieval and larger ΓÇ£parentΓÇ¥ chunks for context?
    A) Parent-Document Retriever
    B) Only Hybrid Search
    C) Only Multimodal
    D) Fixed-k only
  3. What metrics does RAGAS evaluate?
    A) Faithfulness/groundedness, answer relevancy, context recall, Recall@k
    B) Only speed
    C) Only model size
    D) Only chunk size

    B) Naive single retrieval
    C) Only BM25
    D) Only images

Medium Questions

    B) Only images
    C) Only graphs
    D) No verification

    A) Layer improvements: hybrid ΓåÆ HyDE ΓåÆ parent-child ΓåÆ GraphRAG ΓåÆ agentic
    B) Never evaluate
    C) Use only naive RAG
    D) Retrain every day
  3. In the advanced code snippet, what splitter is used for parent-child?

    B) Only one splitter
